coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon dots modified electrode for electrochemical sensing of paclitaxel as an important anticancer drug
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wenze
Song, Bin
Li, Shoushuai
coredata.dc:description: Cancer represents a prominent health concern on a global scale and stands as a significant contributor to mortality. The accurate quantification of anti-cancer drugs such as paclitaxel in human biofluids is critical for effective treatment and monitoring. In this study, a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method was presented for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs), eliminating the need for any additional decoration or modification, by utilizing combretum micranthum extract as a natural carbon source. A paclitaxel sensor was fabricated by modifying a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with the green synthesized CDs. At optimal conditions, the CDs-GCE exhibited a linear response for paclitaxel analysis in a range of 0.07 µM to 35 µM, with a low detection limit of 2.1 nM. The suggested sensor indicates acceptable reproducibility for paclitaxel detection ((RSD=2.6 %). In addition, the CDs-GCE depicts a good resistant versus common interfering species including flutamide, dopamine, glucose, nilutamide, lactose, tinidazole, ascorbic acid, and L-cysteine. The applicability of the sensor for paclitaxel detection in human serum and human urine samples was effectively assessed. The presented electrochemical sensing protocol for paclitaxel detection offers several merits, including a low limit of detection, fast response time, resistance to interference, and ease of use.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paclitaxel
Carbon dots
Green preparation
Combretum micranthum
Electrochemical sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) PSD, (B) FT-IR, (C) UV-Vis and (D) FL analyses of CDs.
(A) XPS spectrum for prepared CDs via combretum micranthum extract and high-resolution data for (B) C 1 s (c) N 1 s and (d) O 1 s.

(A) EIS spectra and (B) CV responses of bare and modified electrode. (C) CV studies of bare and modified electrodes in presence of paclitaxel. (D) CV responses of paclitaxel oxidation at different scan rates. Inset shows plot of oxidation signal current vs. (scanning rate)1/2.
(A) CV responses as a function of solution pH. (B) Plot of potential oxidation of analyte versus pH (C) CV currents as a function of CDs concentration.
(A) DPV responses for different paclitaxel concentration from 0.07 μM to 35 μM using CDs-GCE; (B) Relationship between DPV current and drug concentration.
(A) Reproducibility study of six CDs-GCE sensor for analysis of at same experimental conditions. (B) Life time study of CDs-GCE stored at ambient conditions for a period of 4 weeks.
A comparison of analytical parameters of different electrochemical assays used for paclitaxel analysis.

Differential pulse voltammetry with carbon paste electrode

Differential pulse voltammetry with double-stranded DNA modified gold electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Influence of potential interferences on DPV signal of CDs-GCE.
Analysis of paclitaxel in biofluids using standard addition protocol.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon dots modified electrode for electrochemical sensing of paclitaxel as an important anticancer drug

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of General Surgery, Central Hospital of Xi'an, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of General Surgery, Central Hospital of Xi'an

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of General Surgery, Central Hospital of Xi'an, Xi’an 710061, Shaanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cancer represents a prominent health concern on a global scale and stands as a significant contributor to mortality. The accurate quantification of anti-cancer drugs such as paclitaxel in human biofluids is critical for effective treatment and monitoring. In this study, a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method was presented for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs), eliminating the need for any additional decoration or modification, by utilizing combretum micranthum extract as a natural carbon source. A paclitaxel sensor was fabricated by modifying a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with the green synthesized CDs. At optimal conditions, the CDs-GCE exhibited a linear response for paclitaxel analysis in a range of 0.07 µM to 35 µM, with a low detection limit of 2.1 nM. The suggested sensor indicates acceptable reproducibility for paclitaxel detection ((RSD=2.6 %). In addition, the CDs-GCE depicts a good resistant versus common interfering species including flutamide, dopamine, glucose, nilutamide, lactose, tinidazole, ascorbic acid, and L-cysteine. The applicability of the sensor for paclitaxel detection in human serum and human urine samples was effectively assessed. The presented electrochemical sensing protocol for paclitaxel detection offers several merits, including a low limit of detection, fast response time, resistance to interference, and ease of use.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer remains a significant public health issue, characterized by high morbidity and mortality rates, with millions of new cases diagnosed globally each year [1]. Chemotherapy, alongside surgery and radiation therapy, is a primary treatment modality for many cancers [2]. Paclitaxel, with its chemical structure shown in Scheme 1, is a highly effective natural anticancer agent [3]. The Taxus cuspidata, acknowledged as an endangered and rare natural anticancer plant, is a valuable source of paclitaxel [4]. It is usually used in the medical treatment of ovarian, breast, and non-small-cell lung cancers [5,6]. Its mechanism of action involves stabilizing microtubules and preventing their depolymerization, thereby inhibiting cell division and inducing apoptosis in cancer cells [7]. The analysis of paclitaxel has been carried out using various methods, including chromatography [8], Raman microscopy [9], fluorescence [10] and chemiluminescence [11]. However, analyzing paclitaxel using these strategies is complex and time-consuming compared to electrochemical sensing methods. The use of electrochemical methods for the analysis of paclitaxel has gained significant attention in recent years [12,13]. This approach offers numerous benefits, such as rapid analysis, high sensitivity, a wide calibration range, good selectivity noise, and the utilization of equipment that is both easy to use and cost-effective [14]. Additionally, the potential for developing miniaturized paclitaxel sensors using electrochemical devices has grown in recent years, underscoring the importance of this strategy for practical uses [15]. The fabrication of an efficient paclitaxel sensor requires the modification of the unmodified electrode surfaces with modifiers that have high catalytic features [16].

The utilization of nanostructured compounds in chemistry for surface modification of electrodes offers significant advantages, enhancing the performance and sensitivity of sensors [17–19]. By incorporating nanomaterials into electrode surfaces, the catalytic activity and electron transfer properties are improved, leading to enhanced detection capabilities and selectivity [20,21]. This innovative approach paves the way for more efficient and reliable electrochemical sensing applications, promising advancements in analytical chemistry and sensor technology [22,23]. Over the past few years, nanosized carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene have gained significant interest in scientific researches, especially within the healthcare applications, due to their unique structural characteristics such as high stability, good electrical conductivity, high surface area and facile surface modification [24–26]. Carbon dots (CDs) have been the focus of extensive research among different carbon nanostructures and are thought to be part of the next generation of carbon materials [24,27]. Owing to their particle size, CDs have a strong fluorescence property [28]. CDs offer advantages such as good functionalization capabilities, superior surface-area-to-volume ratio, high biocompatibility, and tunable optical and electronic properties [29]. CDs have been utilized in various fields, such as biomedical and healthcare applications [30,31], electrochemical sensors [32,33], photocatalysts [34,35], and fluorescence sensors [36,37], due to their remarkable properties. To date, numerous synthesis protocols like microwave-assisted route [38], pulsed laser ablation [39], electrochemical [40] and sonochemical approaches [41] have been utilized for the preparation of CDs. Green fabrication of CDs from natural resources offers a sustainable and cost-effective approach compared to conventional manufacturing techniques [42,43]. Some successful attempts for utilization of CDs modified electrodes in drug analysis have been made earlier. For example, Mathew et al. [17] reported a novel electrochemical sensor based on the modification of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with CDs, which was utilized for sensitive determination of doxycycline in table and water samples. Matteo and co-workers [44] used CDs-chitosan composite an efficient modifier for simultaneous detection of theophylline and caffeine. The sensor was characterized by a three orders of magnitude linearity range and a limit of detection suitable for theophylline and caffeine in tea sample.

In this study, the fabrication of CDs using combretum micranthum extract was presented for the first time. Unlike conventional methods that often require complex procedures and hazardous chemicals, our one-step hydrothermal synthesis method is straightforward, environmentally friendly, and cost-effective. The fabricated CDs were fully characterized using different instruments such as UV-Vis spectroscopy, particle size distribution (PSD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and fluorescence (FL). The green prepared CDs were employed as efficient modifier in the fabrication of paclitaxel sensor. The CDs modified electrode depicts notable electrocatalytic performance toward paclitaxel oxidation. The established electrochemical device demonstrated notable sensitivity, a commendable ability to resist interference, and acceptable reproducibility in analysis of paclitaxel in human biofluids. Moreover, this is the first time that such green prepared CDs have been used for voltammetric monitoring of paclitaxel anti-cancer drug.

Dry leaves of combretum micranthum were obtained from local market. The source of the alumina slurry used in this study was Beuhler (USA). All reagents used in this study were purchased from Merck unless otherwise stated. Milli-Q water was used for preparation of all test solutions.

Voltammetric (cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV)) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies were conducted on a computer-controlled OrigaState100 workstation equipped with a three-electrode system. TEM image was acquired using a PHILIPS CM200 FEG instrument. UV-Vis absorption data was obtained using a Cary 50 spectrophotometer. XPS measurement was recorded on a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha XPS system. The FT-IR spectrum was collected using a Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer with KBr disk method. FL spectrum was obtained on a F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). PSD of CDs was also explored with a Zetasizer Nano ZS/ZEN3690 (Malvern, England).

The first step involved washing 3.0 g of grounded combretum micranthum leaves thoroughly under running distilled water (DW). The washed leaves were then blended with 150 mL of DW to produce a juice. The resulting mixture was filtered using Whatman filter paper to obtain a clear solution. Afterward, the mixture was placed into a Teflon-lined pressure vessel and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 4 h. The dark brown solution of CDs obtained was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to eliminate insoluble particles. Following filtration, the CDs solution was further purified by dialysis against deionized using dialysis tubing for overnight. Finally, the purified CDs were freeze-dried. A portion of CDs was fully dispersed into in ultrapure water to prepare a stock solution for further tests. The stock solution was stored in a refrigerator.

Prior to surface modification, the glassy carbon electrode was mechanically polished with alumina slurries to obtain a clean surface. The cleaned GCE was further rinsed with Milli-Q water to eliminate any residual alumina particles. Following the cleaning process, 15 µL of homogenous suspension containing CDs (3.0 mg mL−1) was dropped onto (15 µL) onto the electrode surface and then allowed to air-dry under ambient conditions. The obtained CDs-GCE was subjected into an electrochemical cell for further electro-analytical experiments.

The morphological features of the as prepared CDs were examined using TEM analysis, as shown in Fig. 1. Analysis of the TEM image reveals the green prepared CDs have spherical shape morphology. The PSD analysis ( Fig. 2A) also confirms the fabrication of CDs with average particle size of 4 nm.

To gain further insight into the structure of the CDs, the FT-IR spectrum was measured and analyzed. Fig. 2B reveals a broad band at approximately 3470 cm−1, which owing to the stretching vibrations of the O-H (N-H) bonds [45]. The intense signal detected at 1622 cm−1 signifies the stretching vibration of the CO bond in CDs structure [46]. The FT-IR spectrum reveals a band at 1425 cm−1, which can be due to the stretching vibrations of the O-CO functional groups [47].

The UV-Visible absorption spectrum of the CDs is depicted in Fig. 2C, revealing their optical absorption behavior. Absorption peaks at 273 nm and 321 nm are attributed to electronic transitions from π → π* and from n → π* states, respectively [48]. The FL spectrum of the prepared CDs (Fig. 2D) also demonstrates an emission maximum centered at 522 nm under excitation at 320 nm.

The elemental composition of the CDs was examined using XPS. Fig. 3A shows the full-range XPS spectrum, which detected the presence of O, C, and N in the fabricated modifier. Fig. 3B presents the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum, which can be deconvoluted into four distinct peaks. These peaks, located at 284.4 eV, 285.1 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.2 eV, correspond to the C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, and O–CO bonds, respectively. The N 1 s spectrum in Fig. 3C depicts two peaks at 399.6 eV and 400.6 eV, corresponding to C-N-C and N-H bonding. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3D) shows two major peaks at 531.7 eV and 532.9 eV, which are consistent with the presence of C-OH/C-O-C and CO functionalities, respectively [49]. Collectively, these observations provide compelling evidence supporting the successful synthesis of the CDs.

Fig. 4 A displays the EIS results of the unmodified GCE and CDs-GCE in 0.1 M KCl+ 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4- solution. The Nyquist plots revealed a significant decrease in the semicircle diameter for the CDs/GCE compared to the bare electrode. The EIS findings suggesting that the modification of the GCE with CDs facilitated the electron transfer process. In addition, the lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) value for the CDs/GCE owing to the high conductivity and high surface area of the CDs, which provide more active sites for the redox reaction.

In the present study, the electrochemical performance of the CDs/GCE was studied using CV analysis in Fe(CN)6 3-/4- probe solution. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the bare electrode displays a distinct redox peak Subsequent to the modification with CDs, the electrode CDs/GCE showed a superior electrochemical signal for the studied redox couple. This finding can be ascribed to the remarkable electrical conductivity and high specific surface area of the synthesized CDs.

The mechanism of electro-oxidation of the hydroxy group of paclitaxel using a carbon nanotube composite modified GCE has been previously reported [50]. The CV technique was utilized to further assess the efficacy of CDs-GCE in the analysis of paclitaxel. Fig. 4C reveals that the bare GCE exhibits a weak oxidation peak, indicating the presence of potential kinetic limitations to electron transfer at the CDs-GCE surface. After the modification with CDs, the sensor demonstrated a significant electrochemical signal, which can be attributed to the catalytic properties of the bio-synthesized CDs towards paclitaxel oxidation.

CV analysis was also employed to investigate the effect of scan rate on paclitaxel oxidation at the CDs-GCE. The test solution consisted of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 30 µM paclitaxel. The scan rate was varied from 30 to 130 mV s−1, and the resulting CV curves indicated a clear dependence on the scan rate. As visualized in Fig. 4D, a good linear relationship (R2=0.9986) depicted between the peak currents and the square root of the scan rates, with an. This suggests that the oxidation of drug at the CDs-GCE is a diffusion-controlled process [51].

To optimize the solution, the electrochemical signals of the CDs-GCE in the presence of 30 µM paclitaxel at different pH values (ranging from 4.0 to 10.0) were studied ( Fig. 5A). The results show that the CV response currents obtained at the CDs-GCE were significantly affected by the pH of the buffer solution. As seen in Fig. 5A, the highest optimal pH value for the sensor system is found at pH 7.0. Thus, the buffer solutions with pH 7.0 were employed in the subsequent electrochemical analyses.

Fig. 5B illustrates the influence of solution pH on the electrochemical potential associated with paclitaxel oxidation at the CDs-GCE sensor. As the pH level of the solution increases from 5.0 to 9.0, the peak potential shifts towards more negative values. This relationship is described by the linear equation E = 1.521 – 0.0575 pH. Furthermore, the obtained slope (57.5 mV/pH) shows a good agreement with the theoretical value of 58.6 mV/pH [52]. This finding implies that an equal number of protons and electrons are involved in the paclitaxel oxidation reaction [50].

The effect of modifier dose was evaluated by employing different concentrations (from 1.5 to 4.5 mg L−1) of CDs suspension in the fabrication of CDs-GCE. Fig. 5C illustrates that the maximum CV current was obtained with a modifier concentration of 3.0 mg L−1. This enhancement in electrochemical response can be linked to the availability of more catalytic centers for paclitaxel oxidation on the surface of working CDs-GCE. However, further raises in CDs concentration (greater than 3.0 mg L−1) led to a decline in CV signal responses. Thus, a CDs suspension of 3.0 mg L−1 was determined to be the ideal choice for the manufacturing of the paclitaxel sensor to optimize the catalytic efficiency.

To assess the electrochemical sensing efficacy of the CDs-GCE for detecting paclitaxel, DPV analysis was employed. Fig. 6A presents DPV voltammograms for different concentrations of the paclitaxel. The CDs-GCE sensor exhibited excellent electrochemical sensing performance for paclitaxel detection with a linear range of 0.07–35 μM (Fig. 6B). The regression equation derived from this relationship is expressed as I(µA) = 0.4213 C(µM) + 0.7726. By employing the 3σ rule [53], the detection limit of the suggested electrochemical system was determined to be 2.1 nM. The remarkable electroanalytical sensing efficiency towards paclitaxel determination is probably cause by the fast electron transfer kinetics and extraordinary catalytic capability of the bio-inspired prepared CDs. Table 1 provides a comparative data of the analytical features of the current sensing platform with those of previously reported sensors [54–60]. The suggested platform displays a lower detection limit compared to other sensing assays. In order to study the reproducibility of sensor for paclitaxel detection, the relative standard deviation (RSD) value was calculated using the following relationship:
(1) RSD (%) = (σ / μ) × 100
where σ and μ are the standard deviation of the measurements and mean of the paclitaxel measurements for 7 individual CDs-GCEs ( Fig. 7A), respectively. The RSD value for paclitaxel detection using the suggested sensor was found to be 2.6 %. Therefore, the introduced CDs-GCE yields acceptable reproducibility for paclitaxel determination. The long-term stability of the fabricated electrode was investigated during four-week period. Following ambient storage for 4 weeks, the CDs-GCE exhibited minimal signal loss (<5 %) in its CV response towards paclitaxel detection. The inherent chemical stability of the green-synthesized CDs might contribute to the observed long-term stability of the sensor.

The selectivity of the presented electrochemical platform was investigated using the DPV method. The selectivity of CDs-GCE sensor was evaluated by monitoring the changes in the electrochemical response in the presence of various interference species, including flutamide, dopamine, glucose, nilutamide, lactose, tinidazole, ascorbic acid, and L-cysteine, at a 20-fold higher concentration than paclitaxel. The test solution consisted of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 5 µM paclitaxel. The signal current changes for the explored interfaces are presented in Table 2. The finding indicated that the DPV response changes were below 3 % for all examined interferences, demonstrating the anti-interference ability of the CDs-GCE towards paclitaxel detection.

The usefulness of the CDs-GCE sensor in detecting paclitaxel in human biofluids was examined to assess its practical application. For this purpose, the known concentrations of paclitaxel were added to human urine and blood serum samples as part of a spiking and recovery strategy. Before DPV analyses, the samples were diluted with 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). The CDs-GCE sensor was then used to quantify paclitaxel. As indicated in Table 3, the recovery percentages ranged from 97.7 % to 103.0 %, demonstrating the sensor's accuracy in measuring paclitaxel in complex biological matrices.

While the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of CDs using combretum micranthum extract is straightforward and eco-friendly, scaling up the production for industrial applications might present challenges in terms of consistency and quality control. Besides, the use of natural extracts can introduce variability in the properties of the synthesized CDs due to differences in the composition of the plant extract, which may affect the reproducibility of the sensor’s performance. While the study provides empirical evidence of the CDs' effectiveness for paclitaxel detection, a deeper theoretical understanding of the interaction mechanisms between paclitaxel and the CDs-modified electrode could be further explored.

The developed electrochemical sensor for paclitaxel can be integrated into real-time drug monitoring systems in clinical settings. This would allow for precise dosage control and personalized medicine approaches in cancer treatment. Future work can explore the miniaturization of the present sensing approach and adapted for point-of-care devices, enabling rapid and cost-effective drug level monitoring in resource-limited settings. In addition, the principles introduced in this work can be also extended to sensing other important electroactive biomolecules. This broader application can impact fields beyond cancer research, including neurology and metabolic disorders.

This study describes the development of an innovative electrochemical sensing strategy for the determination of paclitaxel in human biofluids, using the modification of surface electrode with green prepared CDs. The fabricated CDs exhibit electrocatalytic property towards oxidation of paclitaxel. This catalytic effect lowers the activation energy required for the oxidation reaction on the electrode surface, thereby amplifying the current response observed during electrochemical measurements. Through systematic optimization of key experimental parameters, including buffer pH and CDs concentration, the sensor achieves high sensitivity. The resulting DPV response current exhibits a linear relationship with paclitaxel concentration over a range of 0.07–35 μM, with a detection limit of 2.1 nM. The applicability of the CDs-GCE was demonstrated through the successful detection of paclitaxel in human urine and blood serum samples, with recovery values between 97.7 % and 103.0 %. The present assay provides significant advantages, including operational simplicity, low expense, high sensitivity, and ability to resist interference. Furthermore, the sensor exhibits acceptable reproducibility and rapid detection capabilities. These collective attributes suggest that the CDs-GCE sensor is a promising tool for paclitaxel analysis in biological fluids.
The general project of Shaanxi Province fundamental research in natural sciences, NO: 2021JQ-403.

Wenze Sun: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Bin Song: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Shoushuai Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
